agent_raw: The six-week pilot result is interesting but does not support network-wide expansion because three confounds prevent causal attribution to the model. First, pilot merchants volunteered and were primarily large retailers with dedicated risk teams — a self-selected group that differs systematically from the rest of the network. Even in the same calendar weeks, comparing these merchants to the remaining network is comparing unlike populations. Second, some pilot merchants adopted stronger checkout authentication during the same period. Improved authentication reduces fraud directly and independently of any scoring model. The pilot cannot separate model contribution from authentication improvement without stratifying by authentication adoption. Third, fraud labels come from chargebacks and investigations on approved transactions only. If pilot merchants changed their approval thresholds using model scores — allowing more transactions through — the comparison of fraud rates across groups with different approval policies is not controlled for this selection. The result may reflect the pilot group's characteristics and concurrent changes rather than model performance. A properly controlled test — randomized assignment within comparable merchants with stable authentication settings — is needed before the expansion decision.
all_issues_raised: Merchant self-selection: pilot group systematically different from network
Concurrent authentication improvements provide alternative fraud reduction mechanism
Approved-only labels sensitive to different approval rate policies across groups
